data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Index Inches Closer To Correction Territory
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 finished the week at its lowest level in over seven months and is now inches away from correction territory, sitting 8.74% off its all-time high from January 27, 2026. The index posted its fifth consecutive weekly loss, its longest streak since 2022, falling 2.1% from last Friday. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been below the 50-day moving average since February 27th, 2026, and below the 200-day moving average since March 19th, 2026. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st, 2025.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, 2025, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.33%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently down 6.96% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is down 1.56% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/29/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Newmont: I'm Abandoning My 'Sell' Rating After The Gold Correction (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have already written some controversial articles with fervent comments, some with a slightly more offensive tone and others just disagreeing with arguments (feel free to comment on this one as well, especially if you have a different view and/or some important point to highlight). Two of them were related to gold miners. The one about Barrick (B) had an incredible 113 comments, and the one about Newmont (NEM) almost 40.

At the time, the gold price was over $5k/oz, and gold miners like Newmont seemed like an obvious choice for the portfolio: conflicts, geopolitical uncertainties and inflation contributed to this. All of this built a scenario for gold price movements of more than 40% in a year and consequently created this feeling of FOMO and that gold and gold proxy stocks were the place to be.

For Newmont specifically, I highlighted 3 reasons why I was avoiding the stock (you can check it here). Now the scenario has already changed a bit. The gold price has already corrected to more normalized levels, and the stock has also opened a margin of safety. Therefore, I am giving an upgrade to hold.

Newmont and Gold Price: What Changed?

If you see the chart below, you will notice that Newmont and other gold miners are a proxy for the gold price, which has fallen ~17% since my last article.

But this news (here) shows that the gold price and even how the gold price affects gold miners is not that simple. Basically, gold (IAU) (GLD) and silver (SLV) fell due to expectations of increased inflation. This is even a bit counterintuitive, since gold protects against inflation.

However, the uncertainties of the conflicts in the Middle East, which increased oil prices and consequently raised inflation expectations, also indirectly influence interest rates. Now the cuts in the FFR are less clear. I discussed this momentum in an article about the Conflict, if you want to go deeper, check it here. Consequently, the Treasury Yield becomes more attractive, and if we think that the USD is still the “safe” currency to be in this type of scenario, this is not exactly good for gold. I mean, do you prefer to have a gold price that could stay stagnant for a year or an American Treasury yielding 4% per year? Not that you cannot have both, but this is a comparison that must be made and thought about.

But up to that point, I am only talking about the gold price. Obviously, this already affects both revenue and the operating leverage of the gold miners. But in fact, this could be a bit worse. This type of extraction industry is very dependent on energy; Newmont, for example, puts several disclaimers in its 10k like the one below:

“Our operations and projects may be adversely affected by rising energy prices or energy shortages.”

After that, it goes deeper into the fact that a lot of electricity is necessary (remote locations), but it is also intensive in “diesel fuel, heavy fuel oil and natural gas”.

In other words, we saw the gold price falling (less revenue), consequently less operating leverage (less margin) and higher energy cost for 2026 (less margin).

And since there were no revisions to the estimates for 2026, this hasn't even appeared in a ‘consensus’ forward P/E yet. The scenario is quite cloudy; perhaps Newmont in the coming quarters will make relevant adjustments to gold price expectations and gold AISC (which basically would be the cost).

But Why An Upgrade For Newmont Then?

Well, I imagine you must be wondering why I am doing an upgrade if the scenario is so bad.

The truth is that the scenario is not “so bad.”

Yes, it is more uncertain, but a correction in the gold price cycle was not a gigantic surprise, at least not for me. Since it was at a historical peak and distant from the mean, a slight regression toward the mean could already have such an effect; it could even be something just technical, like the sale by some institutional players, no super movement would be necessary, etc.

Now with the gold price closer to $4,500, which is what Newmont used as a base assumption for 2026, as counterintuitive as it seems, we have more margin of safety. This is because, if the gold price falls to $4,000, that’s fine, it wasn't that far off, it's not such a large drop, and it won't be a super negative surprise (different from going from $5,000 to $4,000). And besides that, a positive surprise, like Newmont managing to sell gold for ~$4,700 (avg. for 2026), seems much more possible (although still not as likely).

Redoing the calculations, even being much more pessimistic, it is still possible to find more reasonable multiples.

In my last article, it was possible to find proxies of a P/FCF of ~13x and an EV-to-EBITDA of ~9x.

Recalling, Newmont's guidance for 2026 is 5.3Moz of gold production, a Gold AISC of ~$1,680 and a gold price of ~$4.5k/oz.

As I mentioned, let's be a bit more cautious. Let's use $4,000 gold price and a gold AISC of $1,800 (increase of ~8% due to oil prices, loss of operating leverage, etc.).

This still gives us a gold margin of $2,200/oz.

With the production of 5.3 million oz, the proxy for an EBIT stands at ~$11.6 billion. So the implicit EV-to-EBIT for 2026 is 9x.

With development capital included in this, the FCF proxy goes to $10.2 billion. Of course, I don't expect the FCF to stay at $10 billion. We didn't count depreciation, and we didn't count taxes and so on. In 2025, there was an interest expense of about -$200 million, income and mining tax expense of ~-$4.5 billion and a depreciation (which enters positive) of $2.5 billion. So in practice, this FCF depending on working capital, how much debt they will pre-pay, etc., should stay in a range close to $8 billion.

$8 billion of free cash flow would still be a P/FCF of 13x.

Why is 13x attractive now and it wasn't before? Simple, now this is a stressed assumption. I considered an AISC 8% higher than the guidance, and a gold price of $4,000, 11% lower than the guidance. Even so, it is possible to find a price-to-free cash flow of 13x, which basically implies a shareholder yield close to 8%. This (proxy) alone proves that even in this challenging environment, Newmont could still deliver some solid results.

Outside of that, the forward EV-to-EBIT of the last 5 years of NEM was ~13x. I don't think it is as cheap as the market makes it seem (estimates still point to a multiple of 6.6x), but as I mentioned here, in a slightly more conservative scenario, the company can deliver a multiple of 9x, which would still be slightly below the average.

Upside Risks... And Downside Risks

My rating for Newmont is a hold (neutral stance), and for that very reason, I need to make it a little clearer that there are triggers here for both upside and downside movements.

The positive surprises are a little more obvious, as I’ve already mentioned.

We’re talking about a scenario where the gold price returns to $5k, and that’s certainly not impossible given certain catalysts such as rising uncertainty and potential increased buying volume from institutional investors and central banks. There are also other internal factors, such as a normalization of oil prices, which has a positive impact on costs.

All of this would have a positive effect on Newmont stock, a stock rebound here is possible.

And even though it’s a bit more unclear, downside risks also exist. For instance, if central banks start selling their reserves, geopolitical uncertainties subside, and the gold price returns to $3k–$3.5k, this should trigger a correction in Newmont. It’s not the base case, but it could happen.

Bottom Line

I still don't think Newmont is a compelling stock; for that reason, it is not a buy. But now it already seems much more reasonable to me, and I don't think it's worth the cautious and “avoidant” stance I had before. Now even if the gold price is a bit more bearish, Newmont can still deliver reasonable results; that is, we no longer need to count on a bullish scenario to find a decent shareholder yield.

This also implies that in a bullish scenario, the shareholder yield is not just decent, but good enough.

Therefore, I am giving an upgrade from sell to hold for the Newmont stock.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: eBay: Marketplace, With Greater Ambitions
data.description:
At the start of the year, I concluded that eBay (EBAY) was both improving and fully re-rated at the same time. Shares had seen a spectacular re-rating in recent years, albeit from a low valuation base, driven by accelerating sales growth and margin gains.

With shares having risen for the right reasons, the re-rating made me more reserved, with further triggers needed to drive growth for investors. Ever since shares have lost about 5% in the first quarter of this year, amidst some developments taking place, including an acceleration of growth and strategic acquisition of Depop.

This makes me more comfortable with eBay although I look for a better entry level, given concerns about the sustainability of current growth rates reported.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Adding Some Growth

By mid-February, eBay announced that it had reached a deal to acquire Depop from its smaller peer Etsy (ETSY). The company is acquiring the C2C fashion marketplace, which is deeply rooted with the Gen Z and Millennial customer base.

The deal is valued at $1.2 billion in cash. The mobile-first and community-powered fashion marketplace boosted gross merchandise sales to a billion in 2025, with 7 million active customers, 90% of whom are age 34, or younger. These are connected to some 3 million active sellers being active on the platform. This values the business at just over 1 times GMV of a billion, a premium compared to eBay's valuation around 0.5 times GMV, but Depop shows much stronger growth.

The company sees the deal as closing in the second quarter, funded with existing cash holdings. eBay sees some unspecified synergies, touting $10 billion in GMV transacted on its platform in 2025 related to fashion, with leverage in marketing/technology and other services seen from eBay's scale.

Assuming closing in the second quarter, the Depop deal is seen contributing 1-2 points to full-year sales growth, with low-single-digit headwinds seen to non-GAAP operating income, as some additional interest expense will be included as well. Including synergies and anticipated ramp-up accretion to non-GAAP operating income is seen in 2028 and beyond.

Note that eBay is not expected to accept this dilution easily, as the company announced job cuts of 800 (across its own business), this equal to 6% of the workforce.

Adding Growth At A Price

Alongside the deal announcement, eBay reported its 2025 results, a year in which full-year sales rose by 8% to $11.1 billion, yet real momentum was seen towards year-end, with fourth-quarter sales up 15%, or up 13% in constant currency terms. Growth is really driven by pricing and engagement, with the number of active buyers across platforms up just 1%, to 135 million users.

Growth has largely been driven by the US business, which makes up half of gross merchandise volume, up nearly 20%, while international growth was essentially flat. Revenue growth outpaced GMV growth by 5 points, indicating a continued increase in the ¨cut¨ that eBay asks for its services.

Adjusted earnings rose by 13% to $5.52 per share, as GAAP earnings rose by just thirty-one cents to $4.26 per share. With about three-quarters of the adjustments between both earnings metrics related to stock-based compensation expenses, realistic earnings were seen around $4.50 per share in my book. Based on the definition of net cash, one including equity investments, I peg net debt at just about a billion, as this might double following the purchase of Depop.

That is perfectly manageable given the underlying profitability, as eBay has been quite aggressive in buybacks, reported at $2.5 billion in 2025, down from a +$3 billion number in 2024. Momentum is seen continuing, at least in the first quarter of 2026, as no full-year guidance has been provided. Note that the company is gearing more towards dividends after the company hiked the quarterly dividend by 7% to $0.31 per share.

Revenues are seen topping $3.0 billion for the first quarter, compared to a revenue number just shy of $2.6 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Non-GAAP earnings are seen up eighteen cents to a midpoint of $1.56 per share; this bodes well for impressive continued growth in 2026, despite the headwind from some dilution from Depop.

Hence, a $5 earnings per share number for 2026 looks quite realistic. Such earnings power and shares having fallen to $88 make that earnings multiples have fallen from about 20 times to 17-18 times here, this looking rather appealing. At a roughly $41 billion enterprise valuation, the company trades near 4 times sales and a market multiple in terms of earnings.

With few direct peers out there, it is hard to make a one-for-one comparison in terms of valuation multiples. Given the business model, I think that shares deserve a discount to large technology names, but could actually trade at a premium to software names given the AI-induced carnage taking place there.

Growing Relevance & Value Creation

As discussed above, the number of participants being active on the platform has been rather flattish, yet revenues in relation to gross merchandise volumes have been increasing, notably in the US. Despite the momentum in 2025, which is very promising, the issue is that longer-term growth has been rather lackluster, with revenues up just a cumulative 20% over the past decade, trailing inflation.

This modest growth, amidst flattish margins, has really been complemented by a roughly 60% reduction in the share count. Hence eBay was considered a value play and a capital return story at lower valuations, yet the real achievement has been the ignition of growth. This came in part due to pressure from external factors, with activist investor Elliott Management pushing eBay to sell the Classifieds business pre-pandemic, with other non-core activities being sold as well.

This has resulted in more streamlined operations and focus, yet the growth acceleration in 2025 came somewhat unexpectedly to me. Such growth is concentrated in the US operations and is driven by a higher ¨cut¨ taken by eBay, as well as participants getting more active here, yet growth in the number of users has not really been there.

Hence, I have some fears about the composition of growth, although a deal like Depop is designed to change this. With a $1 billion GMV contribution, that adds just over 1% to GMV, yet the growth of the platform might actually boost organic growth of eBay by tens of basis points.

What Now?

With growth rates on the increase, I note that eBay enjoyed a real re-rating from 10 times earnings multiples a couple of years ago to about 20 times earnings this year. A small pullback and improved earnings power made it so that multiples came down to 17-18 times. This followed a strong fourth quarter, solid guidance for the first quarter of 2026, and a strategic deal for Depop, even though it is seen as near-term dilutive.

All of this is positive, but I feel somewhat at unease about the sustainability of growth acceleration and the concentration of growth, and thereby question the longevity of growth.

However, the pace of growth and the valuation setback rapidly improved appeal in my view, and while I am more upbeat compared to the start of the year, I would like to see a bit of a better entry point. I hope to take advantage of the current turmoil, to get involved at levels in the low eighties, with current volatility providing more opportunities left and right.

data.title: Natural Resource Partners: Nearing Debt-Free Status, But Margin Of Safety Shrinks (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Natural Resource Partners L.P. (NRP), I highlighted the company's progress towards their goal of reaching a fortress balance sheet, with robust free cash flow despite the weak market conditions that can potentially unlock significant distributions going forward.

The company continued their aggressive debt repayments, with a solid 2025 performance despite macro weakness, recently jumping even before the ongoing Iran conflict, which is why I’m downgrading NRP to a Buy, with the valuation no longer reflecting a strong buying opportunity and rising near-term risks of a correction due to a potential resolution in the Middle East pushing commodity prices lower.

Fundamentals Improving Despite Weakness

As they highlight, Q4 was solid overall, generating $168.75 million in free cash flow throughout the year while repaying a very nice $109 million in debt, with $33 million left to retire under their plan to reach a fortress balance sheet that I mentioned before.

After this goal is achieved, they should focus on higher dividends, buybacks, and other opportunistic investments, as they highlight during their presentations.

Meanwhile, back in late February, the company also announced a delay in distribution increases from August probably to November as a result of the $39.2 million soda ash JV investment (back in February, so not included in the financials) that delays their debt repayment goals, although I wouldn't necessarily consider this bad. They invested this to reduce the outstanding amounts under its credit facility and position it to better compete in this tough environment.

However, that report came a couple of days before the strikes against Iran and the subsequent escalation that boosted thermal coal, which can help them reach this a bit sooner, although I wouldn't count on some major boost from it, with the President & COO mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

I want to say what we've said before, though in prior calls, and that is that the longer the bear market continues for all three of our key commodities, the greater the likelihood something happens that pushes that timing back. But right now, that's what the timing looks like.

At worst, this delay at least proves the part of the thesis of investing in this for the long-term yield once they reach the target, and a delay as a result of this kind of investment shouldn’t be seen as a negative, potentially standing to benefit from a cyclical recovery in the industry, while the environment has been weak in all segments of the business.

Alongside the weak prices in mineral rights and carbon neutral initiatives, they mention soda ash as being down due to significant oversupply from new Chinese capacity that kept sale prices below the cost of production for most producers, with the company not expecting any distribution from their Sisecam Wyoming until high-cost capacity is forced to retire (not in the foreseeable future, as they say).

Financially, based on NRP’s latest report, we continue to see significant improvements, with the company reducing their debt significantly and still holding about $30.1 million in cash and cash equivalents.

The base distributions remain relatively modest at $0.75 per quarter, or about 2.41% based on current prices, but this will likely not be the case for long, as mentioned above. They also announced a special distribution of $0.12, which is significantly below the specials seen before, with 2025's $1.21 and 2024's $2.44 being 10 or even 20 times better.

However, this is clearly explained by the environment, and it’s nice to know the kind of special distributions they can pay during great times. Since they paid $56.44 million in distributions in 2025, there is plenty of room for them to increase it alongside potential buybacks or investments, especially once commodity prices potentially recover thanks to factors they mention as well.

Iran Boost May Be Short-Lived

Another aspect of the Iran conflict that may not be as popular in the news is the effect it has on thermal coal, which is once again proving its relevance in this environment, performing nearly identically to oil despite not being in the spotlight as much.

However, given the implications of a prolonged conflict, as mentioned in this Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) Strong Buy analysis posted recently, I don’t view this as something that would last for that long due to how much it would cost from several points of view.

Even if this turns out to be a prolonged conflict despite what Marco Rubio said very recently - calling this something that would last for weeks longer, not months, and highlighting that it might indeed be over relatively soon - it’s important to think about the broader effects it would have on the economy.

If this lasts for too long, then a recession is probably inevitable, while a quick resolution would allow the weak fundamentals of oil, gas, coal, fertilizers, etc. to take over once again - with both of them potentially pushing prices lower in the near-term, which is why I believe investors shouldn’t be happy with the near-term gain seen in the industry.

Still, don’t expect this to immediately recover, as these war premiums could persist for longer given the damage that Iran’s counterattacks had on its neighbors - as the ECB chief also warned - potentially leading to more uncertainty regarding the Fed’s next moves, with the ECB showing its willingness to hike rates as early as next month, pushing European borrowing costs to the highest level in 15 years. Just imagine what a similar stance from the Fed would have on treasury yields, which already skyrocketed since the initial attacks.

As a note, when seeing headlines such as “country X has Y days of oil reserves left” - on top of it sometimes being just propaganda - it’s important to keep in mind that it doesn’t mean the country is going to run out of fuel from that point.

There are many things they can do well before reaching that point, with Australia for example considering measures such as relaxing fuel quality standards, price monitoring, import facilitation measures, among others, while coal also becomes an option in many cases.

Although it might likely be short-lived, I’ll also highlight that this can bring some more attention back to coal and its still important role in the foreseeable future, potentially helping NRP and even allowing them to capitalize on their assets better.

Risks Reiterated

I’ll emphasize that we’re ultimately talking about commodities that are now seeing the fallout of huge spikes in recent years (for now), and the company doesn’t expect significant fundamental improvements in the foreseeable future, which is why expectations of a quick recovery in the market and massive yields shouldn’t be high, as this can still go south at least in the near-term especially as the Iran conflict would de-escalate.

Meanwhile, regardless of this conflict, the market dynamics remain weak, which is why their move of becoming a debt-free financial fortress is smart, but there’s only so much value they can generate out of their assets if the weak environment persists.

Valuation

For NRP’s DCF to Equity valuation, assuming the same weak prices as in 2025, I’ll start with a DCFE of $135 million in 2026 to take into account the repayment of the remainder of their debt obligations, followed by a jump of $30 million in 2027 alongside a CAGR of 3% from 2027 to 2035 (note that this is quite weak even if they just put half of their money into treasuries; much better potential from other investments or diversification), with a 2% terminal growth rate.

To be more conservative despite their soon-to-be spotless balance sheet given the industry, let’s demand a much better 10% discount rate, with 13.31 million units outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $2.11 billion, for an intrinsic value of $158.32 per unit, still above the current $124.50, with current prices reflecting almost perfectly the value we’d get if we input a 12% discount rate.

Conclusions

NRP is a Buy, with the valuation still implying a solid margin of safety that takes into account the weak environment, with significant potential for ramped-up returns in the near future.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industry we’re talking about and its long-term risks, but the valuation remains compelling, with massive executives/board ownership on top - they mention 25% of outstanding units in their latest presentation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fastly: The Future Is Bright, But Watch For These Threats
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Edge cloud specialist Fastly, Inc. (FSLY) has racked up huge gains for shareholders in the past several weeks. What’s more, earnings estimates for this year forecast triple-digit growth, with double digit gains in 2027.

I am bullish on the company, but see there are operational, legal, and short-interest threats that shareholders should watch.

After analyzing the threats and opportunities, I conclude Fastly is a Buy.

About Fastly

The company operates an edge cloud platform which it states, in its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings report, “helps the world’s top brands deliver online experiences that are fast, safe, and engaging through edge compute, delivery, security, and observability offerings that improve site performance, enhance security, and empower innovation at global scale.”

Edge cloud refers to cloud-computing services that are positioned close to the user, rather than in a centralized cloud platform that might be a continent away. For example, a smart factory might have a cloud located on its premises, close to where the data is created and consumed. As the Fastly statement above suggests, edge cloud services can be faster, more secure, and use less bandwidth than centralized cloud services.

More Growth Ahead

Fastly’s net income and basic EPS were slammed by the COVID-19 pandemic, although revenue kept growing steadily:

Since bottoming out in the first quarter of 2022, net earnings and EPS have rebounded robustly. Basic EPS, for example, rose from minus $2.01 in Q1-2022 to minus $0.83 at the end of Q4-2025.

However, the price did not begin to recover until February 6, 2026, and then it rose rapidly:

The price is up, and on March 25, 2026, it was the 52-week high at $29.79, but has since slipped back to $27.43 (at the close on March 27). And looking at management’s guidance and Wall Street estimates, I expect the price to keep climbing.

In the Q4 and full year 2025 earnings report, management guidance pointed to total revenue of $700 - $720 million, up from $624 million in 2025. Using the midpoint of guidance as a reference, that implies a gain of 13.78% this year.

Fastly management also estimated Non-GAAP net income per share of $0.23 - $0.29. For full-year 2026, the midpoint of $0.26 would be a 100% increase.

Wall Street analysts expect earnings of $0.28 per share, but the difference of $0.02 may be due to the analysts’ use of normalized earnings. They also expect double digit gains next year, before falling to high single-digit increases in 2028 and 2029:

Overall, it seems clear both management and Wall Street analysts are bullish on the company’s prospects, as am I. However, there are three areas that investors should watch in succeeding quarters.

Operational Threats

As a networking company, Fastly is expected to be on, all the time. But on June 8, 2021, it experienced a major outage, one that had serious implications. Among the casualties were the United Kingdom’s government website, news network CNN, and Amazon.com (AMZN).

According to various reports, a software deployment made on May 12, 2021 had a bug that was not discovered until a customer accidentally triggered it on June 8. It was immediately fixed and there has been no major failure since then.

However, there have been smaller outages, including one in the Asia Pacific region on March 7, 2023 and another on September 12, 2022 in North America. Fastly observed in its 10-K for 2025, “These outages have resulted and may in the future result in service level agreement claims.”

Future outages, if any, could hurt its reputation or financial results (including legal costs and higher insurance rates). And, the bigger the company gets, the greater the exposure. For example, revenue in Q4-2025 was $172.61 million; that works out to $1.92 million per day in a 90-day quarter.

Other operational threats include customer concentration, staffing, capital intensity, and competition.

Regarding customer concentration, Fastly’s top 10 customers accounted for 32% of its revenue in 2025. The biggest top-10 customer was TikTok, and according to its 10-K, uncertainty about its ownership prospects was a significant source of concern. At the time the 10-K was written (likely in February 2026), TikTok continued to be a customer. Supposing a top-10 customer left, hypothetically, the revenue loss could be 5.0%, which would be the equivalent of losing $8.63 million in Q4.

Staffing represents another area of concern, since Fastly needs highly educated personnel, and there are limited numbers of these professionals. The firm also notes in its 10-K that it sees significant competition for sales representatives, including sales management and sales engineers. AI may help it mitigate lower-skilled shortages, but any failure in recruiting or training high value-added personnel could hurt its top and bottom lines.

Capital intensity: In full-year 2025, Fastly had total capital expenditures of $48.64 million, down from $51.36 million in 2024, but still more than half of net cash provided by operating activities ($94.44 million). The company also put $162.66 million into research and development in 2025, Combine capex with R&D for a total of $211.13 million, and it takes a big chunk (68.71%) of its gross profit of $356.20 million. Yet, the firm has no choice about making these investments because if it does not it could lose ground to its competitors.

Those competitors include Cloudflare, Inc. (NET) and Akamai Technologies, Inc. (AKAM) and Amazon Web Services. All three are larger than Fastly (market cap $4.34 billion); Akamai’s market cap is $16.99 billion, Cloudflare’s is $73.96 billion, and Amazon Web Services is a subsidiary of Amazon.com, Inc.

Those size differentials show up in their core capital expenditures for 2025:

  • Fastly: $28.7 million
  • Akamai: $537.7 million
  • Cloudflare: $315.6 million.

Of course, size alone does not provide a full picture, since much depends on how and where the capex is allocated. Very often, small companies can generate higher returns on their investments. But is that the case in this situation? Judging by the following chart, it has not:

While Akamai and Cloudflare currently have better margins we can see Fastly has been improving more quickly than Akamai, while Cloudflare has been slipping.

Legal Threats

According to the Risk Factors section of the 10-K for 2025, Fastly has had several shareholder lawsuits in the past few years. While none of them appear to have resulted in damages against the company, they are an issue. The company noted in the 10-K, "Lawsuits are time-consuming and expensive to resolve and they divert management’s time and attention. Although we carry insurance, our insurance may not cover potential claims of this type or may not be adequate to indemnify us for all liability that may be imposed."

The company, and its competitors, are vulnerable in multiple ways. Its large customers may seek compensation for down time or system failures. Its shareholders might argue the company did not disclose factors leading to network problems, for example.

Much of its revenue is associated with traffic volume, not fixed contracts. That leads to revenue and earnings volatility and the potential for missing guidance targets.

Its technology is highly advanced, opening the door to potential problems that customers or shareholders might argue should have been anticipated. Its increasing use of AI also drives up risk exposure.

Regulators also may be a threat, if they feel Fastly has not met their compliance standards. In the Risk Factors section of the 10-K, the company noted,

“These laws and regulations may involve privacy and data security, intellectual property, competition, consumer protection, critical infrastructure or other subjects. Many of the laws and regulations to which we are subject are still evolving and being tested in courts and could be interpreted in ways that could harm our business. In addition, the application and interpretation of these laws and regulations often are uncertain, particularly in the new and rapidly evolving industry in which we operate.”

As a multinational corporation, the company also must comply with the many regulations in many other countries.

Short Interest

The following table shows, after the close on March 27, that Fastly has a high level of short interest:

Anything more than mid-single-digits deserves investor attention, since a significant number of shareholders expect the price to fall in the short to medium-term.

At 10.57%, it is high, but then again not so notable when we consider Akamai’s short interest sits at 10.06%. Cloudflare’s, by contrast, is low at 2.66%.

Why this discrepancy? It appears that investors have positioned them differently in their minds.

Cloudflare estimates provided by Wall Street analysts indicate strong and steady growth over the next five years, with EPS growth ranging from 19.51% to 29.22%. Turning to Akamai, the analysts expect slippage of 3.44% this year, followed by five years of earnings growth ranging from 10.53% to 21.26%. For Fastly, the estimates range from 113.33% in 2026 to 13.33% in 2029, a reflection on its volatility. In summary, Cloudflare appears to offer the steadiest earnings growth.

We also get an insight into the Fastly situation from a January 16, 2026 Seeking Alpha report on a Citi assessment.

Regarding Fastly, Seeking Alpha reported on January 16, 2026 that Citi analyst Fatima Boolani was neutral on the firm. It cited him as saying,

“With continued improvements in sales efficiency, our views on business fundamentals/execution also improve and see opportunity for the company to yield balanced estimate surprises, which should support [low-single-digit] EV/S’26 valuation for an asset sustaining [mid-single-digit] topline CAGR that is concurrently delivering profitability improvements.”

However, Boolani added,

“While the turnaround story is becoming increasingly intriguing, recent execution missteps, large customer headwinds, new c-suite (CEO, CFO, CRO), customer concentration magnitudes, lack of scale/differentiation/breadth/brand visibility in security, and the continued cash burn (albeit improving) tempers our enthusiasm and keeps us Neutral rated.”

There are plausible reasons, then, for some shareholders to expect lower prices ahead. Yet, we also know there also are reasons to be bullish.

On The Positive Side

For some investors, the main reason to be optimistic starts with the Wall Street analysts’ view, nine of them, that earnings will increase 113.33% this year. If that happens, the bottom line would transition from a loss ($0.83 per share in 2025) to a profit of $0.28 per share. That would not only be a significant advance, but would also change perceptions in the investment community.

If price follows earnings, which it may be starting to do now, shareholders could look forward to robust capital gains:

That growth is in line with management guidance, which, as we saw above, was for Non-GAAP net income per share of $0.23 - $0.29.

With negative earnings, so far, price multiples are unavailable. And perhaps if Fastly becomes profitable this year the multiples will be high.

However, we should make a distinction between the price multiples and the PEG ratio, which introduces growth into price and earnings relationships. If earnings do grow by over 100% this year, or even by half that amount, the PEG ratio is likely to be favorable (a ratio of less than 1.00 indicates the stock is undervalued).

Fastly's cash runway supports the bullish case. In the second quarter of 2025, free cash flow per share transitioned from negative to positive:

As the chart shows, FCF continued to grow in the second half of 2025, and I see no reason to think it might not continue in 2026 and beyond.

The following table summarizes cash flows over the past three years (in millions of dollars):

Management reported in the Q4 and full-year earnings report that its bottom line on free cash flow was positive: $45.809 million.

Note how cash flow from operations has increased, and as long as that remains positive, Fastly will have a long cash runway.

Also giving credence to a bullish stance are the results of the fourth quarter and full-year 2025. Both set records for revenue, gross margin, and operating profit. As the revenue-netincome-eps chart above showed, Fastly has grown its revenue, net income, and basic EPS impressively over the past five years:

Rating

Based on its past, current and forecast results, I expect Fastly to keep growing, and in 2026 and beyond, to grow profitably. I reach that conclusion despite the concerns noted.

Therefore, I give it a Buy rating. If I had fewer concerns about the operational, legal, and short interest threats, I likely would have given it a Strong Buy rating.

Conclusion

In the most recent earnings report, Fastly management argued the company had reached an inflection point in its growth. That claim will be borne out if the firm can deliver a profitable year in 2026.

But, investors should not ignore the threats that could invalidate the profitable growth thesis and be cautious in setting earnings or price targets.

data.title: CEF Market Weekly Review: Aberdeen Proposes Removing Fund Term Structure
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of March. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Most CEF sectors were down on the week, following lower Treasuries and equities. Only MLPs and Loans finished in the green.

The median CEF sector discount remains wider of its longer-term historic average.

Discounts and discount percentile metrics look most appealing for the sectors below.

We suggested in 2024 that when the Fed starts to cut the policy rate, loan CEFs will begin to cut distributions (blue line) and this will drive its then expensive valuation relative to the rest of the CEF market (green line) significantly cheaper. This is indeed what has happened though a part of the cheapening of loan CEFs likely also has to do with a broader concern about "cockroaches" and the impact of AI disruption on software companies.

Market Themes

Aberdeen infrastructure CEF ASGI wants to ditch its term structure with a proposal being put for a shareholder vote. The fund has a scheduled termination date in 2035. Interestingly, the fund’s annual report says there is a provision to hold a tender offer leading into the termination date which would allow the fund to turn into a perpetual fund if there are $100m of assets remaining (out of $600mm+ assets now). This is basically the "best practice" for term funds, one that has been followed by the vast majority of term CEFs.

Trying to do away with a term date is unusual. Even Blackstone simply shifted the term date further into the future rather than simply trying to remove it for BSL. At this point removing the term date doesn’t look like a big deal. ASGI is trading at a small premium so a termination would actually be a drag on returns as the premium would move towards zero over time. We can see this below in the pull-to-NAV metric which shows a negative annual headwind vs. the NAV into the termination.

However, it is nearly guaranteed that at some point in the next 9 years the fund will trade at a discount. It was trading at a discount wider than 15% in 2023. Allocating to a fund at such a point would provide a significant amount of additional return relative to the performance of the NAV if investors can exit at the NAV, whether through a termination or through a tender offer. Cancelling the term date is therefore a way for the manager to remove this source of alpha in order to avoid losing assets.

Aberdeen has been busy snapping up the funds of smaller CEF managers and has been loudly proclaiming itself to be the fifth largest manager in the CEF market. No doubt it doesn’t want to lose its assets to a tender offer which would threaten its AUM and its fees. All in all this is obviously against shareholder interests and it’s a poor sign of Aberdeen’s interest in looking after its shareholders that it’s even doing this.

Market Commentary

A few more CLO Equity CEFs, namely, OCCI, CCIF, EARN, SPMC have released February NAVs. OCCI and EARN had more modest falls of 9% and 11% respectively while CCIF and SPMC saw drops of 23% and 22%.

February marked the worst month for the sector in more than a year.

We may be getting into a situation where the drop in the NAV over the past year has increased leverage levels of the funds to such an extent that further NAV drops will necessitate a sale of assets in order to redeem senior securities.

As CEFs sell their CLO Equity securities, this puts further pressure on prices in what looks to be a fairly fragile environment for the sector. So far we have not seen wholesale reductions in liabilities - EIC and ECC have redeemed their preferreds however they are replacing them with OTC preferreds, though perhaps not fully. OCCI has already filed to redeem Series C and D. SPMC and CCIF are the most vulnerable from a leverage perspective. CCIF and SPMC preferreds have first call dates later in the year with SPMA and CCIF likely to be partially redeemed.

BlackRock mostly-loan CEFs BSL, BGX, BGB updated their distributions. BSL reduced it by 12%, BGX reduced it by 24% while BGB increased by 3%. The BGX cut is largely optical as there was a big hike in the previous quarter. The new distribution is in line with where it was mid last year.

Although the path is noisy, distributions from these funds should continue to move lower over time in line with lower short-term rates. Two of the funds are term funds with a 2027 scheduled termination date. BSL has extended the termination date twice in the past so there is no reason it won’t try to do so again.

Normally, term CEFs will run a tender offer which preserves the economics of a termination by getting investors out at the NAV without actually doing it. And if there are enough assets remaining in the fund (which there usually is) it continues as a perpetual CEF. In this sense, Blackstone is not really delivering on the premise of term CEFs, rather, it’s maximizing assets under management to juice fees.

data.title: SPAB: Why Stepping Out On The Duration Risk Spectrum Now Makes Sense
data.description:
The State Street SPDR Portfolio Aggregate Bond ETF (SPAB) now yields near 4.7% on a yield-to-maturity (YTM) basis. That's more than 1.1 percentage points above the best money market rate you’ll come across. In fact, for the first time in more than three years, both the 2-year Treasury and 10-year Treasury rates are materially above the Fed Funds rate.

What does that mean for investors? Well, there’s now ample excess yield, in my opinion, to step out on the duration-risk curve. Hence, I have a buy rating on the US Aggregate Bond Index, which retail investors can play through SPAB. I had a buy rating on SPAB back in July of 2023, and the fund has returned 11.4% (total return) since then.

2-Year & 10-Year Yields Well Above the Fed Funds Rate

According to the issuer, SPAB seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price and yield performance of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. It is a low-cost ETF that seeks to offer comprehensive exposure to US dollar denominated investment grade bonds, including government bonds, corporate bonds, mortgage pass-through securities, commercial mortgage-backed securities, and asset-backed securities.

SPAB is a medium-sized ETF, now with $9.2 billion in assets under management as of March 27, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low at 3 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is 4.0%. Keep in mind that the YTM is the best proxy for what investors should expect to receive on a go-forward basis.

Share-price momentum has been dreadful lately. The ETF Grade in that category is a weak D+ by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. I’d call out that risk-adjusted returns were actually quite strong from October 2023 through this past February. But sharply higher interest rates in the past four weeks has weighed on the bond index fund. Risk levels are in check, thanks to its high-quality, moderate-duration profile.

From a liquidity perspective, SPAB sports average daily volume north of 3.5 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just two basis points, per SSGA Funds.

So, with a significant yield premium on cash rates, I assert that now is an ideal time to put cash to work in the high-grade US fixed-income market. Yes, this month has been absolutely nasty from a diversification perspective when comparing stock and bond returns.

The Financial Times put out a chart illustrating how the two major asset classes are moving in lock step, akin to what we saw in the bear-market period of 2022. The difference now, however, is that there’s less in the way of “safe” yield to capture in short-term Treasury bills compared to some four years ago.

Stocks & Bonds Moving Together Again

Just how much has the Aggregate Bond Index climbed from a YTM standpoint? For that, I go to WisdomTree’s Daily Dashboard. It reveals that “the Agg” has gone from a 4.1% yield to a 4.7% yield in very short order.

Yes, that’s more than a full percentage point below the absolute peak in late 2022, but it’s well above the long-term average (back to 2013) of 2.57%.

"The Agg" Now Yields 4.71%

Key risks for SPAB and the broader bond market clearly include macroeconomic factors. Specifically, inflation is concerning. Augur Infinity’s US Timely Inflation gauge soared to its hottest mark in four years by the close of last week.

WTI near $100 and Brent crude oil close to $110 have made energy commodities attractive, leaving fixed income out of favor. An upside risk for SPAB is if there is de-escalation in the Iran war, resulting in lower oil prices, tamer inflation expectations, and falling yields.

Inflation Has Shot Higher

Inflation Seen Peaking at 4.1% YoY This Year

All the while, US GDP growth appears fine for now. The Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model points to Q1 real GDP near 2%. Though recession fears may be on the rise, most data point to ongoing economic expansion.

Higher GDP growth generally puts upward pressure on yields, while softer macro indicators tend to result in lower interest rates.

Solid US Real GDP Growth Expected

For now, macro eyes remain on the Fed Funds future market. There has been wild volatility in this market, with odds for an October rate hike ranging from, call it, 20% on the low side lately and 80% on the high side.

The higher those probabilities go, the worse it is for something like SPAB.

Fed Rate May Stay On Hold Through 2026

But as a reminder, SPAB is a high-grade, moderate-duration fixed-income play. The effective duration is 5.9 years. What that means is that for every instant 1% rise in market rates, you can expect SPAB to drop by 5.9%.

It also means that if an investor buys today and holds it 5.9 years, the effective annual return will be close to the current YTM of 4.7%.

SPAB: Credit Quality & Duration Profiles

Wrapping up with seasonal trends, April has tended to deliver modestly negative returns over the past 10 years (price only, not including dividends).

The May through July stretch has typically been the best period.

SPAB: May-July Bullish Trends

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on SPAB. The low-cost aggregate bond ETF features a now-attractive YTM of 4.7%, particularly compared to money market rates, savings account rates, and even near-dated Treasurys.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report, March 29, 2026
data.description:
Now that the S&P 500 has closed firmly below the -5% line, the next level of support is the -10% line, which is roughly 6300. What began as an orderly pullback has turned into a fear-driven slide. Today we’ll go through the charts to find out where most of the selling is happening, and which parts of the market are holding up the best.

S&P 500 Last 4 Weeks

I had to rescale this chart for the second time in two weeks to accommodate the market’s rapid decline. This chart clearly shows the downdraft we’ve been in for the last 4 weeks. And for now, there doesn't seem to be any relief on the near-term horizon.

Zoom out to 12 months

We’re almost at the end of March and the S&P 500 is down 7.4%. That’s the worst monthly decline we have seen in the last 12 months. You have to go all the way back to September 2022 to find a worse monthly showing.

S&P 500 drawdowns

You can see on this chart how the decline has accelerated. The only positive thing I can say about this is that we are about due for an oversold bounce. The question is, will you sell the rally, or buy the dip? If you have a written investment policy, you already know the answer.

A look at the bull run since 2022.

Here is another chart that is calling out for an oversold bounce. We haven’t reached the 15% drawdown that we experienced one year ago, but we could get there if this Iran war drags on.

Major index performance last week

This chart has some valuable information for us. The Mag 7 had another bad week, which dragged down the Nasdaq 100 as well as the full Nasdaq index. That was expected. Foreign markets held up better than domestic ones – again, not a big surprise. But the interesting thing is the way small, mid, and micro-cap stocks actually went up last week. That’s where the big money was going, and large caps were the source of funds.

Major asset class performance.

Bitcoin had another down week, losing 6%. This dragged down the blockchain companies. The only bright spot was commodities, helped by a surging oil price.

Equity sector performance

As expected, energy was the top performing sector last week. Materials also gained, along with the traditional defensive sectors – utilities and consumer staples. Communication Services took a beating as Meta Platforms (META) dropped 11.5% after losing a landmark lawsuit.

Factor returns

There were only two factors that gained ground lasts week – small caps and dividend yield stocks. This reinforces what we saw on the Indices chart above.

Growth vs. Value returns

Value was clearly where the money was flowing last week, as investors sought to de-risk their portfolios by dumping their growth names.

Commodities returns

Oil is getting all the attention these days, but last week it was Natural Gas that rose the most. There has been a global shift, especially in Europe, away from coal and oil and towards natural gas. This has been going on for a few years.

Non-U.S. markets

S. Korea has a very volatile equity market. They have alternated from the top performing to the bottom performing market several times over the past couple of months. On a YTD basis, though, they are still the global leader – up 24%. Brazil is exhibiting the same kind of volatility, but is only up 15% YTD.

Equity group performance

Looking at the groups, we see small beating large caps, defensive beating cyclical stocks, and value beating growth. The Mag 7 had a dismal week – down 4.9%.

The S&P Mag 7+

META and Alphabet (GOOGL) were subject to heavy selling on the heels of the lawsuit that META lost. The lawsuit sets a precedent that these social media companies can be held liable for the damage they can cause to the mental health of their users.

The S&P Top 7+ Share of Gains.

The S&P 500 is now down 7% YTD, and nearly all of that is due to the weakness of the Mag 7 stocks. The other 493 stocks are still up, on average.

The ten best-performing ETFs YTD

Energy-related ETFs dominated the leader board, along with metals and mining. But it was Rare Earth metals that took the top spot. This ETF is mostly made up of non-US mining companies. Their largest holding is Albemarle Corp (ALB).

The ten worst-performing ETFs YTD

Cannabis stocks continue to circle the drain as the price of the MSOS ETF is now $3.28. This ETF debuted in 2020 at $25 per share, then ran up to $55 a few months later. Since then, it has been all downhill. It lost 30% in 2021, then lost another 72% in 2022. It broke even in 2023, lost 45% in 2024, and finally turned things around in 2025 when it gained 24%. This year it’s back to its losing ways – down 31% YTD.

The ten best-performing momentum stocks Year-to-Date

Sandisk (SNDK) has given back a large portion of its recent gains but still remains on top of the momentum leader board. The stock is up more than 1000% over just the past 12 months.

Final thoughts

The crosscurrents beneath the surface of the market are clear this week. Investors are offloading large caps, growth, and AI hyperscalers while nibbling at smaller stocks in defensive sectors like utilities and consumer staples. Much of the damage that has been done to the S&P 500 can be traced to the Mag 7 stocks, which are now out of favor with investors. Nvidia (NVDA) peaked at $212 last October and is now in a bear market. At some point, these AI hyperscalers will become too cheap to ignore. We aren’t there yet.

To recap the last week’s action, investors were:

  • Selling large caps and buying mid, small, and microcaps
  • Selling blockchain and buying commodities
  • Selling Communication Services and buying Energy
  • Selling Quality and buying Yield
  • Selling growth and buying value
  • Selling cyclicals and buying defensive names
  • Selling U.S. stocks and buying Foreign stocks
  • Selling computer software & hardware and buying miners
  • Selling Bitcoin and buying the U.S. dollar.

data.title: Procter & Gamble, Johnson & Johnson Among 9 Companies To Announce Dividend Increases In The First Half Of April
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of February, I provided predictions for 10 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in March. In this article I’ll look at another 9 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in the first half of April.

Before we see how my predictions from March fared (you can see the original predictions here) and my predictions for the first half of April, there were a few other long-term dividend growth companies that announced their annual dividend increase in the second half of February:

- Electronic component manufacturer TE Connectivity plc (TEL) announced a 9.9% dividend increase to an annualized $3.12, giving the company a forward yield of 1.54%. This is the company’s 13th year of dividend growth.

- Waste Management (WM) announced a 14.5% increase for its 23rd year of dividend growth. The new annual rate of $3.78 gives the company a forward yield of 1.68%.

- Semiconductor equipment manufacturer KLA Corporation (KLAC) announced its 17th year of dividend growth with a 21.1% boost to an annualized $9.20, giving the company a forward yield of 0.64%.

(All yields are based on stock prices at the market close on Friday, March 27th.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements in March

Best Buy Co., Inc. (BBY) – 22 years

Prediction: 4.2–5.8% increase to $3.96–$4.02

Actual: 1.1% increase to $3.84

Forward yield: 6.10%

The electronics retailer announced a second year of 4-cent annual dividend growth.

Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL)—63 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 2.9–4.8% increase to $2.14–$2.18

Actual: 1.9% increase to $2.12

Forward yield: 2.52%

Dividend growth at the personal care products company continues to slow.

CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE)—12 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 13.4–17.9% increase to $1.52–$1.58

Actual: 16.4% increase to $1.56

Forward yield: 4.20%

The operator of skilled nursing and assisted living facilities continues to compound growth at double-digit percentage rates.

DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. (DKS)—12 years

Prediction: 4.7–6.4% increase to $5.08–$5.16

Actual: 3.1% increase to $5.00

Forward yield: 2.64%

I did expect dividend growth at the sports retail chain to decline as Dick’s Sporting Goods digests its acquisition of Foot Locker, but I expected a slightly larger boost this year.

General Dynamics Corporation (GD)—35 years

Prediction: 4.3–5.7% increase to $6.26–$6.34

Actual: 6.0% increase to $6.36

Forward yield: 1.83%

The aerospace & defense contractor continues to compound its payout at 5–6%.

Globe Life Inc. (GL)—21 years

Prediction: 5.6–7.4% increase to $1.14–$1.16

Actual: 22.2% increase to $1.32

Forward yield: 0.98%

It turns out that I missed this announcement at the end of February. The insurer announced a massive 22% increase, building on last year’s 12% boost.

Horace Mann Educators Corporation (HMN)—17 years

Prediction: 2.8–5.7% increase to $1.44–$1.48

Actual: 2.8% increase to $1.44

Forward yield: 3.40%

The financial company announced a sixth year of 4-cent dividend growth.

Kadant Inc. (KAI)—13 years

Prediction: 4.4–8.8% increase to $1.42–$1.48

Actual: 5.9% increase to $1.44

Forward yield: 0.50%

It’s the second year of 6% dividend growth from the specialty industrial manufacturer.

Oxford Industries, Inc. (OXM)—6 years

Prediction: 0–0.7% increase to $2.76–$2.78

Actual: 1.4% increase to $2.80

Forward yield: 8.06%

The apparel manufacturer beat my expectations, but with falling EPS, this year’s boost isn’t much.

Shoe Carnival, Inc. (SCVL)—13 years

Prediction: 6.7–8.3% increase to $0.64–$0.65

Actual: 13.3% increase to $0.68

Forward yield: 4.24%

Despite falling earnings, the shoe retailer’s outstanding financials—no debt and a low payout ratio—mean investors will receive 13% more dividends this year.

Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM)—20 years

Prediction: 13.6–15.2% increase to $3.00–$3.04

Actual: 15.2% increase to $3.04

Forward yield: 1.73%

The owner of the Williams-Sonoma, Pottery Barn, and West Elm furniture store brands continues to compound its payout at double-digit rates.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in the First Half of April

There are 9 long-term dividend growth companies that I expect to announce annual increases in the first half of April. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Procter & Gamble Co. (PG)—69 years of dividend growth

Just two weeks after its primary competitor, Colgate-Palmolive, announced its 63rd year of dividend growth, household and personal products company Procter & Gamble is set to announce its 70th year of dividend growth. P&G has paid dividends since 1890 and began growing them since 1956. When P&G increases its dividend in the first half of April, it will become one of only six publicly traded companies to have grown its payout for seven decades.

The company has 5 reportable business segments: Beauty, which includes hair, personal, and skin care and provides 18% of net sales; Grooming, which includes razors and related appliances and provides 8% of net sales; Health Care, which includes oral care, pain relief, and vitamins and provides 14% of net sales; Fabric & Home Care, providing 36% of net sales with laundry and dishwashing products; and Baby, Feminine & Family Care, covering the remaining 24% of sales.

Despite flat sales in 2025, the company still delivered GAAP EPS growth of 8% and adjusted EPS growth of 4%. This year, P&G is looking at low-single-digit sales and adjusted EPS growth, with EPS growth guidance ranging from flat earnings to 4% growth. In addition to dividend payments, the company returns its free cash flow to investors through share repurchases; P&G has already completed $3.5 billion of the $5 billion in share repurchases planned for the year. With the continuing share repurchases and the adjusted EPS growth this year and next, I expect dividend growth to slow slightly from last year’s 5% boost. In fact, 5% is the highest increase that I think investors will see this year.

Prediction: 3.0–5.0% increase to $4.354–$4.4386

Predicted Forward Yield: 3.05–3.11%

And here are my predictions for 8 other long-term dividend growth companies that should announce annual increases in the first half of April:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
AON plc (AON) 14 Financial - Insurance Brokers 10.1%–12.8% $3.28–$3.36
Despite flat EPS from 2022 to 2024, the Ireland-based insurance broker has consistently compounded its payout by around 10% over the last 5 years. EPS growth picked up in 2025, with AON posting adjusted EPS growth of 9% over the prior year. With this level of EPS growth and a payout ratio below 20%, I expect the company to announce another double-digit percentage boost, just a little above 10%. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.05–1.07%
American Water Works Company, Inc. (AWK) 17 Utilities - Regulated Water 6.9%–9.1% $3.54–$3.61
The New Jersey-based utility announced that it will be merging with Essential Utilities (WTRG). The all-stock merger has been approved by both companies’ shareholders and still needs regulatory approvals before it closes in the first quarter of 2027. Both companies are long-term dividend growers, and American Water Works will likely take Essential Utilities’ 34-year dividend growth history. American Water reaffirmed its long-term EPS and dividend growth targets of 7-9% when it reported earnings in February. And while EPS growth—at 5.6%—fell short this year, investors can still expect American Water to announce a payout boost in line with its long-term target. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.59 - 2.64%
Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) 21 Consumer Defensive - Discount Stores 11.5%–13.1% $5.80–$5.88
This $450 billion market cap retailer seems immune to the law of large numbers. Despite a massive reach and more than a quarter trillion dollars in sales, Costco continues to grow its EPS at double-digit percentage rates, with 2023’s 8% EPS growth being the sole exception in the last decade. This rapid EPS growth supports an excellent compounded dividend growth rate of 13% over the last decade. Investors will see another good year of dividend growth as the company announced 10% EPS growth in 2025. I expect another 12% dividend increase this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.59–0.60%
H.B. Fuller Company (FUL) 55 Basic Materials - Specialty Chemicals 5.3%–7.4% $0.99–$1.01
Adhesives company H. B. Fuller saw adjusted EPS jump 10% last year despite declining revenues, as the company repurchased shares. Looking forward to 2026, the company is projecting 6–7% adjusted EPS growth despite flat revenues. Fuller traditionally boosts its dividend in the 6–7% range, and with adjusted EPS growth right there, I think that’s what investors will see this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.67 - 1.71%
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 63 Healthcare - Drug Manufacturers 5.8%–6.9% $5.50–$5.56
With the spinoff of its consumer brands businesses into Kenvue (KVUE) in 2023, Johnson & Johnson became a pure-play pharmaceutical company. JNJ posted 6% sales growth and 8% adjusted EPS growth in 2025, with sales growth spread evenly across the world and between the drug manufacturer’s two business segments, on the strength of a good drug pipeline. The company’s looking forward to another good year, guiding to another year of 6% sales growth and 7% adjusted EPS growth. While last year’s 5% boost was a little lower than normal, I think with the good earnings growth, the company's dividend boost will be slightly higher than last year’s. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.29 - 2.31%
Southern Company (SO) 24 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.7%–3.4% $3.04–$3.06
The Atlanta-based utility consistently compounds its payout by 3% annually. Although the company’s GAAP EPS have flattened out, adjusted EPS are still growing in the mid-single-digit percentages, the difference being a charge to close out some of Southern Company’s debt. Although 6% adjusted EPS growth would allow for a larger dividend increase, I expect the company to stick with a 3% increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 3.18 - 3.20%
Sonoco Products Company (SON) 42 Consumer Cyclical - Packaging & Containers 1.9%–3.8% $2.16–$2.20
The packaging and container company continues to revamp its business, completing the sale of its ThermoSafe business and using the proceeds to pay down debt while reducing the number of operating segments. Dividend growth has suffered as the company focuses on simplifying its business operations; Sonoco has boosted its annual payout by less than 2% for two years straight, which is below the previous growth rate of 4–5%. Adjusted EPS grew more than 16% in 2025, which would bode well for dividend growth, but the company provided guidance of growth slowing to 5% in 2026. On top of that, Sonoco still has roughly $2.5 billion in debt still to pay off, so I expect the company will announce another small boost this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.06 - 4.13%
Travelers Companies, Inc. (TRV) 23 Financial - Insurance, Property & Casualty 5.0% - 6.3% $4.62 - $4.68
The insurance company is growing earnings at a rapid clip, with net and core income up 28% year-over-year. Compared to Travelers’ EPS of $27.43, the current annual dividend means there’s lots of room for dividend growth. However, Travelers focuses on buying back shares and has reduced the number of outstanding shares by nearly 14% over the last 5 years. The company generally grows its dividend in the mid-single-digit percentages, and I think Travelers will keep doing that as it continues to buy back shares. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.62–1.64%

Summary

After a flurry of dividend announcements in January and February, the pace of increases slowed down in March, as it does every year. But investors received good news from several companies in March, with four companies announcing 10%+ increases. Health care REIT CareTrust, specialty retailers Williams-Sonoma and Shoe Carnival, and insurer Globe Life all announced increases of at least 13%. In particular, Globe Life boosted its dividend by more than 20%. S&P Dividend Aristocrat and defense contractor General Dynamics extended its dividend record to 35 years, while Dividend King Colgate-Palmolive announced a 2% boost for its 63rd year of dividend growth.

We will see more announcements of dividend increases in April, with many of them coming at the end of the month. In the first two weeks of April, investors can expect 10%+ increases from insurer AON and warehouse retailer Costco. We’ll also see Dividend Kings Procter & Gamble and H. B. Fuller announce increases, with P&G hitting the 70-year mark. Packaging company Sonoco Products will extend its dividend growth record to 43 years, and electric utility Southern Company will establish itself as an S&P Dividend Aristocrat with its 25th year of dividend growth.
